speaker: interviewer
text: --number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, you had been talking about your work in America and your marriage to Marie. Can you tell me about your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have two children. One is born in Sweden, Neil-- came here-- he was 5-years-old. And my daughter is born here in this country. And she is in Georgia. And my son is in Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What effect do you think that your experiences have had on the way you lived your life and the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life affected me from these camps, what I was and what I saw. I become less religious, because I don't believe. Because what I was saw with my eyes, what's happened, there was no justice. And people was praying to God and to anybody what came. And no help came from no place. Maybe I am wrong. I don't know. But that what I feel. And then is one more thing I become. I become very much liberal. Because when I see somebody says, this is black. This is grey. This is brown. That hurts me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, why? I have only two kinds of people-- good ones and bad ones-- that's all. And how we looks, nobody can-- can pick his color. If he's a good person, what is the difference? Leon, is there anything else from your experiences that you would like to tell us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can tell you many things what I did and went through-- everything-- probably with you now. Like when I went to prison, on the way to prison, when we marched, the Germans shoot to the prisoners. We was a big amount-- maybe 30,000 or more. We was marching. And in middle of the night-- so machine guns-- and they shoot into all of them. I and-- I and 20 people were running in a yard. And we put in-- we went in in a stable. And there when they came in, say [NON-ENGLISH]. And they-- and they make all kinds of reminders of remarks-- that they gonna kill us. And finally, they start to talk to everyone, ask them how much he was, and how long in the army he was, and how long did he do, and did that, how many Germans he shoot, and thing, and that. And who, they figured, speaks German, is a-- is a 1/2 a German. And who speaks only Polish, is a Pollack. And we're two groups. And I told them that I'm German. And I spoke German. And they took me with the Germans. So they took the Germans out. And the Pollacks are killed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they took us in another place where all the people that survived, that shooting, was laying in-- on a yard, on a field. And he said to us, we should lay down and not picking up the head. If we pick up the head, we're gonna shoot. So I laid down there. So one of the guys what was laying, he had a coat. I didn't have any coat. I lost everything on the front. So he covered me. And he say to me, where you coming from? I say, we're on the way when the shooting was. And we run and see. Now they brought us here. He-- I-- what's happened here? He says, they shoot probably 1/2 of us. And then they was looking who is Jewish in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: They start to look around, and maybe somebody told them. And they helped him. And they took all the Jewish people-- should go bury the dead. And they didn't come back anymore either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I know already what's going on. I laid down. And I watched what's gonna happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning I saw a guy from my company. He was not far away. So he knows me-- that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I run away from there. I-- he shouldn't see me. Maybe he not gonna say nothing. Maybe he will. Who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was marching this weekend to Germany, without food, without anything. Every night we were sleeping on the field. And in Germany-- we came to Germany in a-- on a camp, a temporary camp. It was barracks. We went in in the barracks. And the first day, we came in. We laid down. There were 60 people in a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning came in two officers and say to us, are there here some Jews? Should them stand up. So-- and who from the Polish, what can speak German, should come here. So two came down there that spoke German. He say to them-- and he brought us from-- who stand up only three? So I didn't stand up. So then they came and start to look. They took the Polish people. As you go look, everyone dies. They is gonna recognize better who is Jewish. Because I lived with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought up another three-- like me too. And he says to us, so-- so long the Jews gonna stay here, this will be hanged. They should be the cleaners here in the room. They say to that German guy. And they will not be shot, because we need ammunition on the Russian front. We gonna go right away. So-- and the Russian don't want the Jews either. So it's no other way. We have to hang them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he say, who from-- from the Polish people don't have good shoes, or good pants, or good things, should take away from the Jews. And he left. Two officers it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came right away. Hey, give me this. Give me that. Give me the pants. Give me. Some gave him, some not. And he came to me. I say, get the hell out of here. I will not give you nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he start to draw down from me. So I pushed him. He fell on a table. The table turned around, and he fell. So all of them fell over. They want to come to fight me. So one stand up in my defense. And he says, it's-- it's to be ashamed what you do here in this room. And they, those Jewish boys from the Polish army was always fighting for the country, fighting for our land. They was risking their life. Now you doing things what the Germans want you to do. If one of them gonna live over and you know, tell the story in world, how we gonna look? So he say-- went another way. So I sat down. That what I say, you can't make everybody is guilty for what a few do. Because anybody's-- everybody's not bad. Maybe it's a majority. Still, if it's an-- even if it's a majority, you can't say all of them are bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had many experience, what not all of them are bad. Therefore, I never say all of them are bad. Who does things no good, that is his guilt. It's not the guilt of the whole-- the whole people. We see now a Jewish face. We had people what was bad too. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, it's 50 years since the end of the war. What message would you like to leave for your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] To my children. I would show you a message what I gave to children in a school. I was in a school, and I kept a lecture for about three hours. There was about 3,000 kids and teachers-- Nova High School.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a kid stand up and asked me the same question-- what you asking me. What did you learn from all those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, what I learned, I told you. I like that question very much. I learned that everything lays in your hand, kids. You shouldn't make any different between nationality, or color, or religion. You should all love each other like one people from one country. And all are Americans. You should love each other. It will never happen again-- things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the kids knocked. Me about a 1/2 an hour!

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Washington.

speaker: interviewee
text: 144 394 [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who's on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Where it's taken? In-- that was taken in Sweden, maybe-- I came to Sweden in '45, '46-- no, '45. That must be taken in '47, '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who's on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son, Neil, and my-- his wife, Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken about three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Now, ?] [? three ?] years ago. Today we have '95-- '92.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were on vacation. They took this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the Bahamas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, Leon?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Susan. And this is taken also the same time with my son's-- about in-- in '92.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Georgia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
